coaching.subject: acceptance criteria completeness and verifiability

coaching.coaching: Plan how verification of AC3 file-level changes will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: verification of AC3 file-level changes

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

evidence: No `## Acceptance Criteria` section with `- [ ]` items found.
findings.finding: The ticket has no `## Acceptance Criteria` checklist. A plan cannot be reviewed for completion without testable, checkable criteria.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `## Acceptance Criteria` section with `- [ ]` checklist items, one per observable, in-session-verifiable outcome.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit proving commands to AC3, e.g. `grep -c 'config\.py' .github/module-size-allowlist.txt | grep -q '^0'` and `grep -c 'config\.py' .github/module-size-ceilings.txt | grep -q '^0'` (and a grep for the stale arch.md row), so the deletion is machine-verifiable rather than inspection-only.
evidence: AC3 text: 'config.py removed from .github/module-size-allowlist.txt AND .github/module-size-ceilings.txt; the stale config.py remedy row deleted from docs/architecture.md'
No grep/diff/cat command is given to confirm these deletions, unlike every other AC in the list.
findings.finding: AC3 names two file-level changes ('removed from .github/module-size-allowlist.txt AND .github/module-size-ceilings.txt; stale remedy row deleted from docs/architecture.md') but supplies no proving command that would produce evidence of their completion — unlike ACs 1, 2, 4, 5, 6 which all cite a concrete command.
scenarios: An executing agent marks AC3 done by visual inspection without a reproducible check; a reviewer cannot machine-verify the criterion was met.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC3: `grep -v 'config\.py' .github/module-size-allowlist.txt` (should produce no match line), `grep -v 'config\.py' .github/module-size-ceilings.txt` (same), and `grep -v 'config\.py.*split' docs/architecture.md` to confirm the stale row is gone. Or rely on AC4 (`test_module_size_contract.py`) as the machine-verifiable proxy for the allowlist/ceilings files and explicitly state that.

evidence: verify command `wc -l` inspects output with `wc` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable
verify command `wc -l` inspects output with `wc` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief entry to docs/architecture.md (or AGENTS.md) describing _config_sources.py's role (raw-input source layer: filesystem discovery, TOML parsing/cache, env overrides) and the seam with config.py, so the split is captured in a persistent architectural document, not just the ticket.
evidence: Plan says: 'DELETES that stale row rather than claiming it records the seam below'
Plan says AC#3: 'the stale config.py remedy row deleted from docs/architecture.md' — no mention of adding a new row or section
The new module _config_sources.py is an architectural decision (a new layer in the config subsystem) described only in the ticket, not in any persistent doc target
T5e(c): 'A novel architectural decision is captured in an ADR / AGENTS.md / design-doc update'
findings.finding: The plan deletes the stale config.py remedy row from docs/architecture.md but does not add any new entry or section documenting _config_sources.py as a new architectural module and the new raw-input-source seam. Future maintainers reading architecture.md will have no persistent record of why the file exists, what it owns, or how the two-module seam is intended to be maintained.
findings.location: AC#3 / docs/architecture.md section; 'New seam' plan section
scenarios: A maintainer later reads architecture.md and finds no record of the _config_sources / config.py seam, leading to confusion about ownership or accidental re-merging of the modules
A future offender-row audit finds config.py gone but no explanation of the replacement structure
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC#3 step, after deleting the stale remedy row, insert a new row or note in the architecture.md module-registry/design section, e.g.: '_config_sources.py — leaf: raw-input source layer (file discovery, TOML parse cache, env/legacy-alias overrides); imported by config.py which owns typed-merge, precedence, and the resolved-Config cache.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace bare `wc -l` in ACs 1 and 2 with a cross-platform verification form (e.g., `python -c "import pathlib; assert 100 <= len(pathlib.Path('src/rebar/_config_sources.py').read_text().splitlines()) <= 800"`) so Windows developers and CI runners can verify the line-count bounds.
evidence: Windows platform cell of the support matrix has no native `wc` binary; `(Get-Content file).Count` is the PowerShell equivalent.
findings.finding: AC1 and AC2 specify `wc -l` as the in-session verification command for line-count bounds. `wc -l` is a POSIX utility not available in Windows cmd or PowerShell natively, so a developer or CI runner on Windows cannot execute these ACs as written.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria, items 1 and 2: "`python -m pytest … -q` passes" / "`wc -l` in [100, 800]"
scenarios: Windows developer workstation or Windows CI runner: executing `wc -l src/rebar/_config_sources.py` produces ''wc' is not recognized as an internal or external command', making AC1 and AC2 unverifiable as written.

findings.finding: The ticket has no `## Acceptance Criteria` checklist. A plan cannot be reviewed for completion without testable, checkable criteria.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `## Acceptance Criteria` section with `- [ ]` checklist items, one per observable, in-session-verifiable outcome.
